justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6) with correct invariant classifications. Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note lock BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Cancellation rules correctly split into patient (BR-3, invariant: false for the time window) and admin (BR-4, invariant: false), capturing the role boundary. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1, AC-2), double-booking attempt (AC-3, AC-4), late cancellation attempt (AC-5, AC-6, AC-7), and unauthorized record access (AC-8, AC-9). Confidence level of 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism and other ambiguities.

observations.text: All 6 core rules identified with an additional 7th rule (BR-7) for the editable window, showing thoroughness without missing required items
Invariant classifications are precise — regulatory constraints (BR-1, BR-5, BR-6) correctly marked true; operational policies (BR-3, BR-4, BR-7) correctly marked false with clear justifications
Cancellation role boundary is well-captured: BR-3 handles patient constraint, BR-4 handles admin override, and AC-5/AC-6/AC-7 test both sides
12 acceptance criteria significantly exceed the 4 minimum, including boundary cases like adjacent appointments (AC-4) and exact 2-hour boundary (AC-12)
Confidence at 72 appropriately reflects the well-structured but incomplete spec, with specific assumptions about license validation, treating physician scope, and regulatory framework
Edge cases are exceptionally thorough — concurrent booking race condition, license revocation mid-lifecycle, in-flight edit during lock transition, and cancelled appointment notes
Escalations provide concrete options with recommendations, particularly the append-only addenda suggestion for note locking which reflects real healthcare domain knowledge
Ubiquitous language section is comprehensive with 12 well-defined terms that would serve as a genuine domain glossary

trials.justification: All 6 expected rules identified (BR-1 through BR-8 cover all 6 and add nuance by splitting consultation note locking into the time window and immutability aspects). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, record lock immutability BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. Role-based cancellation correctly captured with BR-3 (patient, invariant: false for the time threshold) and BR-4 (admin-only after window, invariant: true for the role boundary). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license verification scope, back-to-back appointment semantics, and treating physician access duration.

observations.text: Exceeded expectations by splitting the consultation notes rule into BR-7 (48h window, invariant: false — correctly noting the time threshold is configurable policy) and BR-8 (immutability of locked notes, invariant: true — correctly identifying the hard integrity constraint). This is a more precise decomposition than the rubric required.
12 acceptance criteria with precise boundary condition tests (e.g., AC-3 back-to-back booking, AC-9/AC-10 testing just before and after the 48-hour mark). Far exceeds the 4 minimum.

Correctly classified BR-3 (2-hour cancellation window) as invariant: false with sound justification that the threshold is configurable policy, while BR-4 (role boundary for late cancellation) is invariant: true.
Rich edge cases section identifying concurrency concerns (simultaneous booking), boundary conditions (exact 2-hour mark), and unspecified scenarios (license revocation, post-completion access).
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the treating physician access scope and the note creation vs. editing distinction after 48 hours.

Confidence level of 72 with clearly articulated assumptions about license verification being a one-time gate and back-to-back appointment semantics — exactly the kind of assumption-noting the rubric requires.

BR-7 classifies the 48-hour consultation note lock as invariant: false. The rubric expects 'record lock' marked invariant: true. However, the agent compensated by splitting into BR-7 (time window = soft) and BR-8 (immutability = hard invariant), which is arguably more precise. This is a defensible decomposition rather than a misclassification.

trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-8 covers all 6 and adds 2 more). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, record lock immutability BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. The 48-hour window itself (BR-7) marked invariant: false with sound justification that the threshold is policy while the mechanism matters. Role-based cancellation correctly captured as separate rules (BR-3 patient, BR-4 admin). 13 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: booking licensed physician (AC-1), double-booking attempt (AC-3), late cancellation attempt (AC-6), unauthorized record access (AC-9), plus boundary cases. Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation scope and boundary semantics.

observations.text: Exceeded rule count: 8 well-structured rules instead of the minimum 6, with BR-8 (note immutability after lock) being a valuable addition that strengthens the regulatory analysis

Excellent invariant classification: cleanly separates regulatory invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-5, BR-6, BR-8) from policy decisions (BR-3 cancellation window, BR-4 admin override, BR-7 48-hour threshold)

BR-7 shows nuanced thinking: the 48-hour window is correctly marked invariant: false (the threshold is policy) while BR-8 captures the immutability of locked notes as invariant: true — this distinction is sophisticated
13 acceptance criteria with excellent coverage including boundary conditions (AC-4 back-to-back, AC-13 exact 2-hour boundary) and negative cases
Rich edge cases section identifies concurrency risks, timezone issues, and write-timing ambiguity for note locking — all operationally critical
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the license revocation gap which is a genuine compliance concern
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated with specific high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions about license validation meaning
